title: Empedocles On Etna - A Dramatic Poem
content:
В В В В PERSONS:
В В В В EMPEDOCLES.
В В В В PAUSANIAS, a Physician.
В В В В CALLICLES, a young Harp-player.

В В В В The Scene of the Poem is on Mount Etna; at first in the forest region,
В В В В afterwards on the summit of the mountain.



В В В В ACT I: SCENE I

В В В В A Pass in the forest region of Etna. Morning

В В В В CALLICLES

В В В В (Alone, resting on a rock by the path)

В В В В THE MULES, I think, will not be here this hour.
В В В В They feel the cool wet turf under their feet
В В В В By the stream-side, after the dusty lanes
В В В В In which they have toilпїЅd all night from Catana,
В В В В And scarcely will they budge a yard. O Pan
В В В В How gracious is the mountain at this hour!
В В В В A thousand times have I been here alone
В В В В Or with the revellers from the mountain towns,
В В В В But never on so fair a morn;the sun
В В В В Is shining on the brilliant mountain crests,
В В В В And on the highest pines: but further down
В В В В Hero in the valley is in shade; the sward
В В В В Is dark, and on the stream the mist still hangs;
В В В В One sees oneпїЅs foot-prints crushпїЅd in the wet grass,
В В В В OneпїЅs breath curls in the air; and on these pines
В В В В That climb from the streamпїЅs edge, the long grey tufts,
В В В В Which the goats love, are jewellпїЅd thick with dew.
В В В В Here will I stay till the slow litter comes.
В В В В I have my harp too, that is well., Apollo!
В В В В What mortal could be sick or sorry here?
В В В В I know not in what mind Empedocles,
В В В В Whose mules I followпїЅd, may be coming up,
В В В В But if, as most men say, he is half mad
В В В В With exile, and with brooding on his wrongs,
В В В В Pausanias, his sage friend, who mounts with him,
В В В В Could scarce have lighted on a lovelier cure.
В В В В The mules must be below, far down. I hear
В В В В Their tinkling bells, mixпїЅd with the song of birds,
В В В В Rise faintly to me, now it stops!, WhoпїЅs here?
В В В В Pausanias! and on foot? alone?

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В  And thou, then?
В В В В I left thee supping with Peisianax,
В В В В With thy head full of wine, and thy hair crownпїЅd,
В В В В Touching thy harp as the whim came on thee,
В В В В And praisпїЅd and spoilпїЅd by master and by guests
В В В В Almost as much as the new dancing girl.
В В В В Why hast thou followпїЅd us?

В В В В CALLICLES

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В  The night was hot,
В В В В And the feast past its prime; so we slippпїЅd out,
В В В В Some of us, to the portico to breathe;
В В В В Peisianax, thou knowпїЅst, drinks late;and then,
В В В В As I was lifting my soilпїЅd garland off,
В В В В I saw the mules and litter in the court.
В В В В And in the litter sate Empedocles;
В В В В Thou, too, wert with him. Straightway I sped home;
В В В В I saddled my white mule, and all night long
В В В В Through the cool lovely country followпїЅd you,
В В В В PassпїЅd you a little since as morning dawnпїЅd,
В В В В And have this hour sate by the torrent here,
В В В В Till the slow mules should climb in sight again.
В В В В And nowпїЅ?

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В And now, back to the town with speed
В В В В Crouch in the wood first, till the mules have passпїЅd;
В В В В They do but halt, they will be here anon.
В В В В Thou must be viewless to Empedocles;
В В В В Save mine, he must not meet a human eye.
В В В В One of his moods is on him that thou knowпїЅst.
В В В В I think, thou wouldпїЅst not vex him.

В В В В CALLICLES

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В No, and yet
В В В В I would fain stay and help thee tend him; once
В В В В He knew me well, and would oft notice me.
В В В В And still, I know not how, he draws me to him,
В В В В And I could watch him with his proud sad face,
В В В В His flowing locks and gold-encircled brow
В В В В And kingly gait, for ever; such a spell
В В В В In his severe looks, such a majesty
В В В В As drew of old the people after him,
В В В В In Agrigentum and Olympia,
В В В В When his star reignпїЅd, before his banishment,
В В В В Is potent still on me in his decline.
В В В В But oh, Pausanias, he is changed of late
В В В В There is a settled trouble in his air
В В В В Admits no momentary brightening now;
В В В В And when he comes among his friends at feasts,
В В В В пїЅTis as an orphan among prosperous boys.
В В В В Thou knowпїЅst of old he loved this harp of mine,
В В В В When first he sojournпїЅd with Peisianax;
В В В В He is now always moody, and I fear him.
В В В В But I would serve him, soothe him, if I could,
В В В В Dared one but try.

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В Thou wert a kind child ever.
В В В В He loves thee, but he must not see thee now.
В В В В Thou hast indeed a rare touch on thy harp,
В В В В He loves that in thee, too; there was a time
В В В В (But that is passпїЅd) he would have paid thy strain
В В В В With music to have drawn the stars from heaven.
В В В В He has his harp and laurel with him still,
В В В В But he has laid the use of music by,
В В В В And all which might relax his settled gloom.
В В В В Yet thou mayпїЅst try thy playing if thou wilt,
В В В В But thou must keep unseen; follow us on,
В В В В But at a distance; in these solitudes,
В В В В In this clear mountain air, a voice will rise,
В В В В Though from afar, distinctly; it may soothe him.
В В В В Play when we halt, and, when the evening comes
В В В В And I must leave him (for his pleasure is
В В В В To be left musing these soft nights alone
В В В В In the high unfrequented mountain spots),
В В В В Then watch him, for he ranges swift and far,
В В В В Sometimes to EtnaпїЅs top, and to the cone;
В В В В But hide thee in the rocks a great way down.
В В В В And try thy noblest strains, my Callicles,
В В В В With the sweet night to help thy harmony.
В В В В Thou wilt earn my thanks sure, and perhaps his.

В В В В CALLICLES

В В В В More than a day and night, Pausanias,
В В В В Of this fair summer weather, on these hills,
В В В В Would I bestow to help Empedocles.
В В В В That needs no thanks; one is far better here
В В В В Than in the broiling city in these heats.
В В В В But tell me, how hast thou persuaded him
В В В В In this his present fierce, man-hating mood,
В В В В To bring thee out with him alone on Etna

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В Thou hast heard all men speaking of Pantheia,
В В В В The woman who at Agrigentum lay
В В В В Thirty long days in a cold trance of death,
В В В В And whom Empedocles callпїЅd back to life.
В В В В Thou art too young to note it, but his power
В В В В Swells with the swelling evil of this time,
В В В В And holds men mute to see where it will rise.
В В В В He could stay swift diseases in old days,
В В В В Chain madmen by the music of his lyre,
В В В В Cleanse to sweet airs the breath of poisonous streams,
В В В В And in the mountain chinks inter the winds.
В В В В This he could do of old; but now, since all
В В В В Clouds and grows daily worse in Sicily,
В В В В Since broils tear us in twain, since this new swarm
В В В В Of sophists has got empire in our schools
В В В В Where he was paramount, since he is banishпїЅd,
В В В В And lives a lonely man in triple gloom,
В В В В He grasps the very reins of life and death.
В В В В I askпїЅd him of Pantheia yesterday,
В В В В When we were gatherпїЅd with Peisianax,
В В В В And he made answer, I should come at night
В В В В On Etna here, and be alone with him,
В В В В And he would tell me, as his old, tried friend,
В В В В Who still was faithful, what might profit me;
В В В В That is, the secret of this miracle.

В В В В CALLICLES

В В В В Bah! Thou a doctor? Thou art superstitious.
В В В В Simple Pausanias, пїЅtwas no miracle!
В В В В Pantheia, for I know her kinsmen well,
В В В В Was subject to these trances from a girl.
В В В В Empedocles would say so, did he deign;
В В В В But he still lets the people, whom he scorns,
В В В В Gape and cry wizard at him, if they list.
В В В В But thou, thou art no company for him;
В В В В Thou art as cross, as soured as himself.
В В В В Thou hast some wrong from thine own citizens,
В В В В And then thy friend is banishпїЅd, and on that,
В В В В Straightway thou fallest to arraign the times,
В В В В As if the sky was impious not to fall.
В В В В The sophists are no enemies of his;
В В В В I hear, Gorgias, their chief, speaks nobly of him,
В В В В As of his gifted master and once friend.
В В В В He is too scornful, too high-wrought, too bitter.
В В В В пїЅTis not the times, пїЅtis not the sophists vex him;
В В В В There is some root of suffering in himself,
В В В В Some secret and unfollowпїЅd vein of woe,
В В В В Which makes the time look black and sad to him.
В В В В Pester him not in this his sombre mood
В В В В With questionings about an idle tale,
В В В В But lead him through the lovely mountain paths,
В В В В And keep his mind from preying on itself,
В В В В And talk to him of things at hand and common,
В В В В Not miracles; thou art a learned man,
В В В В But credulous of fables as a girl.

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В And thou, a boy whose tongue outruns his knowledge,
В В В В And on whose lightness blame is thrown away.
В В В В Enough of this! I see the litter wind
В В В В Up by the torrent-side, under the pines.
В В В В I must rejoin Empedocles. Do thou
В В В В Crouch in the brush-wood till the mules have passпїЅd;
В В В В Then play thy kind part well. Farewell till night!



В В В В SCENE II

В В В В Noon. A Glen on the highest skirts of the woody
В В В В region of Etna

В В В В EMPEDOCLES. PAUSANIAS

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В The noon is hot; when we have crossпїЅd the stream
В В В В We shall have left the woody tract, and come
В В В В Upon the open shoulder of the hill.
В В В В See how the giant spires of yellow bloom
В В В В Of the sun-loving gentian, in the heat,
В В В В Are shining on those naked slopes like flame!
В В В В Let us rest here; and now, Empedocles,
В В В В PantheiaпїЅs history.В В В В В В В В [A harp-note below is heard.

В В В В EMPEDOCLES

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В Hark! what sound was that
В В В В Rose from below? If it were possible,
В В В В And we were not so far from human haunt,
В В В В I should have said that some one touchпїЅd a harp.
В В В В Hark! there again!

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В пїЅTis the boy Callicles,
В В В В The sweetest harp-player in Catana,
В В В В He is for ever coming on these hills,
В В В В In summer, to all country festivals,
В В В В With a gay revelling band; he breaks from them
В В В В Sometimes, and wanders far among the glens.
В В В В But heed him not, he will not mount to us;
В В В В I spoke with him this morning. Once more, therefore,
В В В В Instruct me of PantheiaпїЅs story, Master,
В В В В As I have prayпїЅd thee.

В В В В EMPEDOCLES

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В That? and to what end?

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В It is enough that all men speak of it.
В В В В But I will also say, that when the Gods
В В В В Visit us as they do with sign and plague,
В В В В To know those spells of time that stay their hand
В В В В Were to live free from terror.

В В В В EMPEDOCLES

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В Spells? Mistrust them.
В В В В Mind is the spell which governs earth and heaven.
В В В В Man has a mind with which to plan his safety;
В В В В Know that, and help thyself.

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В But thy own words?
В В В В пїЅThe wit and counsel of man was never clear,
В В В В Troubles confuse the little wit he has.пїЅ
В В В В Mind is a light which the Gods mock us with,
В В В В To lead those false who trust it.
В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В [The harp sounds again

В В В В EMPEDOCLES

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В Hist! once more!
В В В В Listen, Pausanias!, Aye, пїЅtis Callicles!
В В В В I know those notes among a thousand.В В В В Hark!

В В В В CALLICLES

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В [Sings unseen, from below.
В В В В The track winds down to the clear stream,
В В В В To cross the sparkling shallows; there
В В В В The, cattle love to gather, on their way
В В В В To the high mountain pastures, and to stay,
В В В В Till the rough cow-herds drive them past,
В В В В Knee-deep in the cool ford; for пїЅtis the last
В В В В Of all the woody, high, well-waterпїЅd dells
В В В В On Etna; and the beam
В В В В Of noon is broken there by chestnut boughs
В В В В Down its steep verdant sides; the air
В В В В Is freshenпїЅd by the leaping stream, which throws
В В В В Eternal showers of spray on the mossпїЅd roots
В В В В Of trees, and veins of turf, and long dark shoots
В В В В Of ivy-plants, and fragrant hanging bells
В В В В Of hyacinths, and on late anemonies,
В В В В That muffle its wet banks; but glade,
В В В В And stream, and sward, and chestnut trees,
В В В В End here; Etna beyond, in the broad glare
В В В В Of the hot noon, without a shade,
В В В В Slope behind slope, up to the peak, lies bare;
В В В В The peak, round which the white clouds play.
В В В В In such a glen, on such a day,
В В В В On Pelion, on the grassy ground,
В В В В Chiron, the aged Centaur, lay,
В В В В The young Achilles standing by.
В В В В The Centaur taught him to explore
В В В В The mountains; where the glens are dry,
В В В В And the tired Centaurs come to rest,
В В В В And where the soaking springs abound,
В В В В And the straight ashes grow for spears,
В В В В And where the hill-goats come to feed,
В В В В And the sea-eagles build their nest.
В В В В He showпїЅd him Phthia far away,
В В В В And said: O boy, I taught this lore
В В В В To Peleus, in long distant years!
В В В В He told him of the Gods, the stars,
В В В В The tides;and then of mortal wars,
В В В В And of the life which heroes lead
В В В В Before they reach the Elysian place
В В В В And rest in the immortal mead;
В В В В And all the wisdom of his race.
В В В В [The music below ceases, and EMPEDOCLES speaks,
В В В В В В В В accompanying himself in a solemn manner
В В В В В В В В on his harp.
В В В В The out-spread world to span
В В В В A cord the Gods first slung,
В В В В And then the soul of man
В В В В There, like a mirror, hung,
В В В В And bade the winds through space impel the gusty toy.
В В В В Hither and thither spins
В В В В The wind-borne mirroring soul,
В В В В A thousand glimpses wins,
В В В В And never sees a whole;
В В В В Looks once, and drives elsewhere, and leaves its last employ.

В В В В The Gods laugh in their sleeve
В В В В To watch man doubt and fear,
В В В В Who knows not what to believe
В В В В Since he sees nothing clear,
В В В В And dares stamp nothing false where he finds nothing sure.

В В В В Is this, Pausanias, so?
В В В В And can our souls not strive,
В В В В But with the winds must go,
В В В В And hurry where they drive?
В В В В Is Fate indeed so strong, manпїЅs strength indeed so poor?

В В В В I will not judge! that man,
В В В В Howbeit, I judge as lost,
В В В В Whose mind allows a plan
В В В В Which would degrade it most;
В В В В And he treats doubt the best who tries to see least ill.

В В В В Be not. then, fearпїЅs blind slave
В В В В Thou art my friend; to thee,
В В В В All knowledge that I have.
В В В В All skill I wield, are free;
В В В В Ask not the latest news of the last miracle,

В В В В Ask not what days and nights
В В В В In trance Pantheia lay,
В В В В But ask how thou such sights
В В В В MayпїЅst see without dismay;
В В В В Ask what most helps when known, thou son of Anchitus!

В В В В What? hate, and awe, and shame
В В В В Fill thee to see our world;
В В В В Thou feelest thy soulпїЅs frame
В В В В Shaken and rudely hurlпїЅd.
В В В В What? life and time go hard with thee too, as with us;

В В В В Thy citizens, пїЅtis said,
В В В В Envy thee and oppress,
В В В В Thy goodness no men aid,
В В В В All strive to make it less;
В В В В Tyranny, pride, and lust fill SicilyпїЅs abodes;

В В В В Heaven is with earth at strife,
В В В В Signs make thy soul afraid,
В В В В The dead return to life,
В В В В Rivers are dried, winds stayпїЅd;
В В В В Scarce can one think in calm, so threatening are the Gods;

В В В В And we feel, day and night,
В В В В The burden of ourselves,
В В В В Well, then, the wiser wight
В В В В In his own bosom delves,
В В В В And asks what ails him so, and gets what cure he can.

В В В В The sophist sneers: Fool, take
В В В В Thy pleasure, right or wrong!
В В В В The pious wail: Forsake
В В В В A world these sophists throng!
В В В В Be neither saint nor sophist-led, but be a man.

В В В В These hundred doctors try
В В В В To preach thee to their school.
В В В В We have the truth! they cry.
В В В В And yet their oracle.
В В В В Trumpet it as they will, is but the same as thine.

В В В В Once read thy own breast right,
В В В В And thou hast done with fears!
В В В В Man gets no other light,
В В В В Search he a thousand years.
В В В В Sink in thyself! there ask what ails thee, at that shrine!

В В В В What makes thee struggle and rave?
В В В В Why are men ill at ease?,
В В В В пїЅTis that the lot they have
В В В В Fails their own will to please;
В В В В For man would make no murmuring, were his will obeyпїЅd.

В В В В And why is it, that still
В В В В Man with his lot thus fights?,
В В В В пїЅTis that he makes this will
В В В В The measure of his rights,
В В В В And believes Nature outraged if his willпїЅs gainsaid.

В В В В Couldst thou, Pausanias, learn
В В В В How deep a fault is this!
В В В В Couldst thou but once discern
В В В В Thou hast no right to bliss,
В В В В No title from the Gods to welfare and repose;

В В В В Then thou wouldst look less mazed
В В В В WheneпїЅer from bliss debarrпїЅd,
В В В В Nor think the Gods were crazed
В В В В When thy own lot went hard.
В В В В But we are all the same, the fools of our own woes!

В В В В For, from the first faint morn
В В В В Of life, the thirst for bliss
В В В В Deep in manпїЅs heart is born;
В В В В And, sceptic as he is,
В В В В He fails not to judge clear if this be quenchпїЅd or no.

В В В В Nor is that thirst to blame!
В В В В Man errs not that he deems
В В В В His welfare his true aim,
В В В В He errs because he dreams
В В В В The world does but exist that welfare to bestow.

В В В В We mortals are no kings
В В В В For each of whom to sway
В В В В A new-made world up-springs
В В В В Meant merely for his play;
В В В В No, we are strangers here; the world is from of old.

В В В В In vain our pent wills fret,
В В В В And would the world subdue.
В В В В Limits we did not set
В В В В Condition all we do;
В В В В Born into life we are, and life must be our mould.

В В В В Born into life, man grows
В В В В Forth from his parentsпїЅ stem,
В В В В And blends their bloods, as those
В В В В Of theirs are blent in them;
В В В В So each new man strikes root into a far fore-time.

В В В В Born into life, we bring
В В В В A bias with us here,
В В В В And, when here, each new thing
В В В В Affects us we come near;
В В В В To tunes we did not call our being must keep chime.

В В В В Born into life, in vain,
В В В В Opinions, those or these,
В В В В UnalterпїЅd to retain
В В В В The obstinate mind decrees;
В В В В Experience, like a sea, soaks all effacing in.

В В В В Born into life, who lists
В В В В May what is false hold dear,
В В В В And for himself make mists
В В В В Through which to see less clear;
В В В В The world is what it is, for all our dust and din.

В В В В Born into life, пїЅtis we,
В В В В And not the world, are new.
В В В В Our cry for bliss, our plea,
В В В В Others have urged it too;
В В В В Our wants have all been felt, our errors made before.

В В В В No eye could be too sound
В В В В To observe a world so vast,
В В В В No patience too profound
В В В В To sort whatпїЅs here amassпїЅd;
В В В В How man may here best live no care too great to explore.

В В В В But we, as some rude guest
В В В В Would change, whereпїЅer he roam,
В В В В The manners there professпїЅd
В В В В To those he brings from home,
В В В В We mark not the worldпїЅs course, but would have it take ours.

В В В В The worldпїЅs course proves the terms
В В В В On which man wins content;
В В В В Reason the proof confirms;
В В В В We spurn it, and invent
В В В В A false course for the world, and for ourselves, false powers.

В В В В Riches we wish to get,
В В В В Yet remain spendthrifts still;
В В В В We would have health, and yet
В В В В Still use our bodies ill;
В В В В Bafflers of our own prayers, from youth to lifeпїЅs last scenes.

В В В В We would have inward peace,
В В В В Yet will not look within;
В В В В We would have misery cease,
В В В В Yet will not cease from sin;
В В В В We want all pleasant ends, but will use no harsh means;

В В В В We do not what we ought,
В В В В What we ought not, we do,
В В В В And loan upon the thought
В В В В That chance will bring us through;
В В В В But our own acts, for good or ill, are mightier powers.

В В В В Yet, even when man forsakes
В В В В All sin,, is just, is pure,
В В В В Abandons all which makes
В В В В His welfare insecure,
В В В В Other existences there are, that clash with ours.

В В В В Like us, the lightning fires
В В В В Love to have scope and play;
В В В В The stream, like us, desires
В В В В An unimpeded way;
В В В В Like us, the Libyan wind delights to roam at large.

В В В В Streams will not curb their pride
В В В В The just man not to entomb,
В В В В Nor lightnings go aside
В В В В To leave his virtues room;
В В В В Nor is that wind less rough which blows a good manпїЅs barge.

В В В В Nature, with equal mind,
В В В В Sees all her sons at play
В В В В Sees man control the wind,
В В В В The wind sweep man away;
В В В В Allows the proudly-riding and the founderпїЅd bark.

В В В В And, lastly, though of ours
В В В В No weakness spoil our lot,
В В В В Though the non-human powers
В В В В Of Nature harm us not.
В В В В The ill-deeds of other men make often our life dark.

В В В В What were the wise manпїЅs plan?,
В В В В Through this sharp, toil-set life,
В В В В To fight as best he can.
В В В В And win whatпїЅs won by strife.
В В В В But we an easier way to cheat our pains have found.

В В В В ScratchпїЅd by a fall, with moans
В В В В As children of weak age
В В В В Lend life to the dumb stones
В В В В Whereon to vent their rage.
В В В В And bend their little fists, and rate the senseless ground;

В В В В So, loath to suffer mute.
В В В В We, peopling the void air,
В В В В Make Gods to whom to impute
В В В В The ills we ought to bear;
В В В В With God and Fate to rail at, suffering easily.

В В В В Yet grant, as sense long missпїЅd
В В В В Things that are now perceivпїЅd,
В В В В And much may still exist
В В В В Which is not yet believпїЅd,
В В В В Grant that the world were full of Gods we cannot see;

В В В В All things the world which fill
В В В В Of but one stuff are spun,
В В В В That we who rail are still,
В В В В With what we rail at, one;
В В В В One with the oпїЅer-labourпїЅd Power that through the breadth and length

В В В В Of earth, and air, and sea,
В В В В In men, and plants, and stones,
В В В В Hath toil perpetually,
В В В В And struggles, pants, and moans
В В В В Fain would do all things well, but sometimes fails in strength.

В В В В And patiently exact
В В В В This universal God
В В В В Alike to any act
В В В В Proceeds at any nod,
В В В В And quietly declaims the cursings of himself.

В В В В This is not what man hates,
В В В В Yet he can curse but this.
В В В В Harsh Gods and hostile Fates
В В В В Are dreams! this only is;
В В В В Is everywhere; sustains the wise, the foolish elf.

В В В В Nor only, in the intent
В В В В To attach blame elsewhere,
В В В В Do we at will invent
В В В В Stern Powers who make their care
В В В В To embitter human life, malignant Deities;

В В В В But, next, we would reverse
В В В В The scheme ourselves have spun,
В В В В And what we made to curse
В В В В We now would lean upon,
В В В В And feign kind Gods who perfect what man vainly tries.

В В В В Look, the world tempts our eye,
В В В В And we would know it all!
В В В В We map the starry sky,
В В В В We mine this earthen ball,
В В В В We measure the sea-tides, we number the sea-sands;

В В В В We scrutinize the dates
В В В В Of long-past human things,
В В В В The bounds of effacпїЅd states,
В В В В The lines of deceasпїЅd kings;
В В В В We search out dead menпїЅs words, and works of dead menпїЅs hands;

В В В В We shut our eyes, and amuse
В В В В How our own minds are made,
В В В В What springs of thought they use,
В В В В How rightenпїЅd, how betrayпїЅd;
В В В В And spend our wit to name what most employ unnamпїЅd;

В В В В But still, as we proceed.
В В В В The mass swells more and more
В В В В Of volumes yet to read,
В В В В Of secrets yet to explore.
В В В В Our hair grows grey, our eyes are dimmпїЅd, our heat is tamed.

В В В В We rest our faculties,
В В В В And thus address the Gods
В В В В пїЅTrue science if there is,
В В В В It stays in your abodes;
В В В В ManпїЅs measures cannot mete the immeasurable All;

В В В В пїЅYou only can take in
В В В В The worldпїЅs immense design,
В В В В Our desperate search was sin,
В В В В Which henceforth we resign,
В В В В Sure only that your mind sees all things which befall!пїЅ

В В В В Fools! that in manпїЅs brief term
В В В В He cannot all things view.
В В В В Affords no ground to affirm
В В В В That there are Gods who do!
В В В В Nor does being weary prove that he has where to rest!

В В В В Again: our youthful blood
В В В В Claims rapture as its right;
В В В В The world, a rolling flood
В В В В Of newness and delight,
В В В В Draws in the enamourпїЅd gazer to its shining breast;

В В В В Pleasure to our hot grasp
В В В В Gives flowers after flowers,
В В В В With passionate warmth we clasp
В В В В Hand after hand in ours;
В В В В Nor do we soon perceive how fast our youth is spent.

В В В В At once our eyes grow clear;
В В В В We see in blank dismay
В В В В Year posting after year,
В В В В Sense after sense decay;
В В В В Our shivering heart is mined by secret discontent;

В В В В Yet still, in spite of truth,
В В В В In spite of hopes entombпїЅd,
В В В В That longing of our youth
В В В В Burns ever unconsumпїЅd,
В В В В Still hungrier for delight as delights grow more rare.

В В В В We pause; we hush our heart,
В В В В And then address the Gods:
В В В В пїЅThe world hath failпїЅd to impart
В В В В The joy our youth forbodes,
В В В В FailпїЅd to fill up the void which in our breasts we bear.

В В В В пїЅChangeful till now, we still
В В В В LookпїЅd on to something new;
В В В В Let us, with changeless will,
В В В В Henceforth look on to you,
В В В В To find with you the joy we in vain here require!пїЅ

В В В В Fools! that so often here
В В В В Happiness mockпїЅd our prayer,
В В В В I think, might make us fear
В В В В A like event elsewhere!
В В В В Make us, not fly to dreams, but moderate desire!

В В В В And yet, for those who know
В В В В Themselves, who wisely take
В В В В Their way through life, and bow
В В В В To what they cannot break,
В В В В Why should I say that life need yield but moderate bliss?

В В В В Shall we, with temper spoilпїЅd,
В В В В Health sappпїЅd by living ill,
В В В В And judgement all embroilпїЅd
В В В В By sadness and self-will,
В В В В Shall we judge what for man is not true bliss or is?

В В В В Is it so small a thing
В В В В To have enjoyпїЅd the sun,
В В В В To have lived light in the spring,
В В В В To have loved, to have thought, to have done;
В В В В To have advancпїЅd true friends, and beat down baffling foes;

В В В В That we must feign a bliss
В В В В Of doubtful future date,
В В В В And, while we dream on this,
В В В В Lose all our present state,
В В В В And relegate to worlds yet distant our repose?

В В В В Not much, I know, you prize
В В В В What pleasures may be had,
В В В В Who look on life with eyes
В В В В EstrangпїЅd, like mine, and sad;
В В В В And yet the village churl feels the truth more than you,

В В В В WhoпїЅs loath to leave this life
В В В В Which to him little yields;
В В В В His hard-taskпїЅd sunburnt wife,
В В В В His often-labourпїЅd fields,
В В В В The boors with whom he talkпїЅd, the country spots he knew.

В В В В But thou, because thou hearпїЅst
В В В В Men scoff at Heaven and Fate,
В В В В Because the Gods thou fearпїЅst
В В В В Fail to make blest thy state,
В В В В Tremblest, and wilt not dare to trust the joys there are.

В В В В I say: Fear not! Life still
В В В В Leaves human effort scope.
В В В В But, since life teems with ill,
В В В В Nurse no extravagant hope;
В В В В Because thou must not dream, thou needпїЅst not then despair!

В В В В В В В В [A long pause. At the end of it the notes of a
В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В harp below are again heard, and CALLICLES
В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В sings:,

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В Far, far from here,
В В В В The Adriatic breaks in a warm bay
В В В В Among the green Illyrian hills; and there
В В В В The sunshine in the happy glens is fair,
В В В В And by the sea, and in the brakes.
В В В В The grass is cool, the sea-side air
В В В В Buoyant and fresh, the mountain flowers
В В В В As virginal and sweet as ours.
В В В В And there, they say, two bright and aged snakes,
В В В В Who once were Cadmus and Harmonia,
В В В В Bask in the glens or on the warm sea-shore,
В В В В In breathless quiet, after all their ills.
В В В В Nor do they see their country, nor the place
В В В В Where the Sphinx lived among the frowning hills,
В В В В Nor the unhappy palace of their race,
В В В В Nor Thebes, nor the Ismenus, any more.

В В В В There those two live, far in the Illyrian brakes.
В В В В They had stayпїЅd long enough to see,
В В В В In Thebes. the billow of calamity
В В В В Over their own dear children rollпїЅd,
В В В В Curse upon curse, pang upon pang,
В В В В For years. they sitting helpless in their home,
В В В В A grey old man and woman; yet of old
В В В В The Gods had to their marriage come,
В В В В And at the banquet all the Muses sang.

В В В В Therefore they did not end their days
В В В В In sight of blood; but were rapt, far away,
В В В В To where the west wind plays,
В В В В And murmurs of the Adriatic come
В В В В To those untrodden mountain lawns; and there
В В В В Placed safely in changed forms, the Pair
В В В В Wholly forget their first sad life, and home,
В В В В And all that Theban woe, and stray
В В В В For ever through the glens, placid and dumb.

В В В В EMPEDOCLES

В В В В That was my harp-player again!, where is he?
В В В В Down by the stream

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В Yes, Master, in the wood.

В В В В EMPEDOCLES

В В В В He ever loved the Theban story well!
В В В В But the day wears. Go now, Pausanias,
В В В В For I must be alone. Leave me one mule;
В В В В Take down with thee the rest to Catana.
В В В В And for young Callicles, thank him from me;
В В В В Tell him I never failпїЅd to love his lyre
В В В В But he must follow me no more tonight.

В В В В PAUSANIAS

В В В В Thou wilt return to-morrow to the city?

В В В В EMPEDOCLES

В В В В Either to-morrow or some other day,
В В В В In the sure revolutions of the world,
В В В В Good friend, I shall revisit Catana.
В В В В I have seen many cities in my time
В В В В Till my eyes ache with the long spectacle,
В В В В And I shall doubtless see them all again;
В В В В Thou knowпїЅst me for a wanderer from of old.
В В В В Meanwhile, stay me not now. Farewell, Pausanias!
В В В В В В В В В В В В [He departs on his way up the mountain.

В В В В PAUSANIAS (alone)

В В В В I dare not urge him further; he must go.
В В В В But he is strangely wrought!, I will speed back
В В В В And bring Peisianax to him from the city;
В В В В His counsel could once soothe him. But, Apollo!
В В В В How his brow lightenпїЅd as the music rose!
В В В В Callicles must wait here, and play to him;
В В В В I saw him through the chestnuts far below,
В В В В Just since, down at the stream., Ho! Callicles!
В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В [He descends, calling.



В В В В ACT II

В В В В Evening. The Summit of Etna

В В В В EMPEDOCLES

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В Alone!,
В В В В On this charrпїЅd, blackenпїЅd, melancholy waste,
В В В В CrownпїЅd by the awful peak, EtnaпїЅs great mouth,
В В В В Round which the sullen vapour rolls, alone
В В В В Pausanias is far hence, and that is well,
В В В В For I must henceforth speak no more with man.
В В В В He has his lesson too, and that debtпїЅs paid;
В В В В And the good, learned, friendly, quiet man,
В В В В May bravelier front his life, and in himself
В В В В Find henceforth energy and heart; but I,
В В В В The weary man, the banishпїЅd citizen,
В В В В Whose banishment is not his greatest ill,
В В В В Whose weariness no energy can reach,
В В В В And for whose hurt courage is not the cure,
В В В В What should I do with life and living more?

В В В В No, thou art come too late, Empedocles!
В В В В And the world hath the day, and must break thee,
В В В В Not thou the world. With men thou canst not live,
В В В В Their thoughts, their ways, their wishes, are not thine;
В В В В And being lonely thou art miserable,
В В В В For something has impairпїЅd thy spiritпїЅs strength,
В В В В And dried its self-sufficing fount of joy.
В В В В Thou canst not live with men nor with thyself,
В В В В Oh sage! oh sage!, Take then the one way left;
В В В В And turn thee to the elements, thy friends,
В В В В Thy well-tried friends, thy willing ministers,
В В В В And say:, Ye servants, hear Empedocles,
В В В В Who asks this final service at your hands!
В В В В Before the sophist brood hath overlaid
В В В В The last spark of manпїЅs consciousness with words,
В В В В Ere quite the being of man, ere quite the world
В В В В Be disarrayпїЅd of their divinity,
В В В В Before the soul lose all her solemn joys,
В В В В And awe be dead, and hope impossible,
В В В В And the soulпїЅs deep eternal night come on,
В В В В Receive me, hide me, quench me, take me home!
В В В В [He advances to the edge of the crater. Smoke
В В В В В В В В and fire break forth with a loud noise, and
В В В В В В В В CALLICLES is heard below singing:,

В В В В The lyreпїЅs voice is lovely everywhere!
В В В В In the court of Gods, in the city of men,
В В В В And in the lonely rock-strewn mountain glen.
В В В В In the still mountain air.

В В В В Only to Typho it sounds hatefully!
В В В В To Typho only, the rebel oпїЅerthrown,
В В В В Through whose heart Etna drives her roots of stone,
В В В В To imbed them in the sea.

В В В В Wherefore dost thou groan so loud?
В В В В Wherefore do thy nostrils flash,
В В В В Through the dark night, suddenly,
В В В В Typho, such red jets of flame?,
В В В В Is thy torturпїЅd heart still proud?
В В В В Is thy fire-scathпїЅd arm still rash?
В В В В Still alert thy stone-crushпїЅd frame?
В В В В Doth thy fierce soul still deplore
В В В В The ancient rout by the Cilician hills,
В В В В And that curst treachery on the Mount of Gore?
В В В В Do thy bloodshot eyes still see
В В В В The fight that crownпїЅd thy ills,
В В В В Thy last defeat in this Sicilian sea?
В В В В Hast thou sworn, in thy sad lair,
В В В В Where east the strong sea-currents suckпїЅd thee down,
В В В В Never to cease to writhe, and try to sleep,
В В В В Letting the sea-stream wander through thy hair?
В В В В That thy groans, like thunder deep,
В В В В Begin to roll, and almost drown
В В В В The sweet notes, whose lulling spell
В В В В Gods and the race of mortals love so well,
В В В В When through thy eaves thou hearest music swell?

В В В В But an awful pleasure bland
В В В В Spreading oпїЅer the ThundererпїЅs face,
В В В В When the sound climbs near his seat,
В В В В The Olympian council sees;
В В В В As he lets his lax right hand,
В В В В Which the lightnings doth embrace,
В В В В Sink upon his mighty knees.
В В В В And the eagle, at the beck
В В В В Of the appeasing gracious harmony,
В В В В Droops all his sheeny, brown, deep-featherпїЅd neck,
В В В В Nestling nearer to JoveпїЅs feet;
В В В В While oпїЅer his sovereign eye
В В В В The curtains of the blue films slowly meet,
В В В В And the white Olympus peaks
В В В В Rosily brighten, and the soothпїЅd Gods smile
В В В В At one another from their golden chairs,
В В В В And no one round the charmпїЅd circle speaks.
В В В В Only the loved Hebe bears
В В В В The cup about, whose draughts beguile
В В В В Pain and care, with a dark store
В В В В Of fresh-pullпїЅd violets wreathпїЅd and nodding oпїЅer;
В В В В And her flushпїЅd feet glow on the marble floor.

В В В В EMPEDOCLES

В В В В He fables, yet speaks truth.
В В В В The brave impetuous heart yields everywhere
В В В В To the subtle, contriving head;
В В В В Great qualities are trodden down,
В В В В And littleness united
В В В В Is become invincible.

В В В В These rumblings are not TyphoпїЅs groans, I know!
В В В В These angry smoke-bursts
В В В В Are not the passionate breath
В В В В Of the mountain-crushпїЅd, torturпїЅd, intractable Titan king!
В В В В But overall the world
В В В В What suffering is there not seen
В В В В Of plainness oppressпїЅd by cunning,
В В В В As the well-counsellпїЅd Zeus oppressпїЅd
В В В В The self-helping son of earth!
В В В В What anguish of greatness
В В В В RailпїЅd and hunted from the world,
В В В В Because its simplicity rebukes
В В В В This envious, miserable age!
В В В В I am weary of it!,
В В В В Lie there, ye ensigns
В В В В Of my unloved pre-eminence
В В В В In an age like this!
В В В В Among a people of children,
В В В В Who throngпїЅd me in their cities,
В В В В Who worshippпїЅd me in their houses,
В В В В And askпїЅd, not wisdom,
В В В В But drugs to charm with,
В В В В But spells to mutter,
В В В В All the foolпїЅs-armoury of magic!, Lie there,
В В В В My golden circlet!
В В В В My purple robe!

В В В В CALLICLES (from below)

В В В В As the sky-brightening south-wind clears the day,
В В В В And makes the massпїЅd clouds roll,
В В В В The music of the lyre blows away
В В В В The clouds that wrap the soul.

В В В В Oh, that Fate had let me see
В В В В That triumph of the sweet persuasive lyre!
В В В В That famous, final victory
В В В В When jealous Pan with Marsyas did conspire!

В В В В When, from far ParnassusпїЅ side,
В В В В Young Apollo, all the pride
В В В В Of the Phrygian flutes to tame,
В В В В To the Phrygian highlands came!
В В В В Where the long green reed-beds sway
В В В В In the rippled waters grey
В В В В Of that solitary lake
В В В В Where MaeanderпїЅs springs are born;
В В В В Where the ridgпїЅd pine-wooded roots
В В В В Of Messogis westward break,
В В В В Mounting westward, high and higher.
В В В В There was held the famous strife;
В В В В There the Phrygian brought his flutes,
В В В В And Apollo brought his lyre;
В В В В And, when now the westering sun
В В В В TouchпїЅd the hills, the strife was done,
В В В В And the attentive Muses said
В В В В пїЅMarsyas! thou art vanquishпїЅd.пїЅ
В В В В Then ApolloпїЅs minister
В В В В HangпїЅd upon a branching fir
В В В В Marsyas, that unhappy Faun,
В В В В And began to whet his knife.
В В В В But the Maenads, who were there,
В В В В Left their friend, and with robes flowing
В В В В In the wind, and loose dark hair
В В В В OпїЅer their polishпїЅd bosoms blowing,
В В В В Each her ribbonпїЅd tambourine
В В В В Flinging on the mountain sod,
В В В В With a lovely frightenпїЅd mien
В В В В Came about the youthful God.
В В В В But he turnпїЅd his beauteous face
В В В В Haughtily another way,
В В В В From the grassy sun-warmпїЅd place,
В В В В Where in proud repose he lay,
В В В В With one arm over his head,
В В В В Watching how the whetting sped.

В В В В But aloof on the lake strand,
В В В В Did the young Olympus stand,
В В В В Weeping at his masterпїЅs end;
В В В В For the Faun had been his friend.
В В В В For he taught him how to sing.
В В В В And he taught him flute-playing.
В В В В Many a morning had they gone
В В В В To the glimmering mountain lakes,
В В В В And had torn up by the roots
В В В В The tall crested water-reeds
В В В В With long plumes, and soft brown seeds,
В В В В And had carved them into flutes,
В В В В Sitting on a tabled stone
В В В В Where the shoreward ripple breaks.
В В В В And he taught him how to please
В В В В The red-snooded Phrygian girls,
В В В В Whom the summer evening sees
В В В В Flashing in the danceпїЅs whirls
В В В В Underneath the starlit trees
В В В В In the mountain villages.
В В В В Therefore now Olympus stands,
В В В В At his masterпїЅs piteous cries
В В В В Pressing fast with both his hands
В В В В His white garment to his eyes,
В В В В Not to see ApolloпїЅs scorn;
В В В В Ah, poor Faun, poor Faun! ah, poor Faun!

В В В В EMPEDOCLES

В В В В And lie thou there,
В В В В My laurel bough!
В В В В Scornful ApolloпїЅs ensign, lie thou there!
В В В В Though thou hast been my shade in the worldпїЅs heat,
В В В В Though I have loved thee, lived in honouring thee,
В В В В Yet lie thou there,
В В В В My laurel bough!

В В В В I am weary of thee!
В В В В I am weary of the solitude
В В В В Where he who bears thee must abide!
В В В В Of the rocks of Parnassus,
В В В В Of the gorge of Delphi,
В В В В Of the moonlit peaks, and the caves.
В В В В Thou guardest them, Apollo!
В В В В Over the grave of the slain Pytho,
В В В В Though young, intolerably severe;
В В В В Thou keepest aloof the profane,
В В В В But the solitude oppresses thy votary!
В В В В The jars of men reach him not in thy valley,
В В В В But can life reach him?
В В В В Thou fencest him from the multitude,
В В В В Who will fence him from himself?
В В В В He hears nothing but the cry of the torrents
В В В В And the beating of his own heart.
В В В В The air is thin, the veins swell,
В В В В The temples tighten and throb there,
В В В В Air! air!

В В В В Take thy bough; set me free from my solitude!
В В В В I have been enough alone!

В В В В Where shall thy votary fly then? back to men?,
В В В В But they will gladly welcome him once more,
В В В В And help him to unbend his too tense thought,
В В В В And rid him of the presence of himself,
В В В В And keep their friendly chatter at his ear,
В В В В And haunt him, till the absence from himself,
В В В В That other torment, grow unbearable;
В В В В And he will fly to solitude again,
В В В В And he will find its air too keen for him,
В В В В And so change back; and many thousand times
В В В В Be miserably bandied to and fro
В В В В Like a sea-wave, betwixt the world and thee,
В В В В Thou young, implacable God! and only death
В В В В Shall cut his oscillations short, and so
В В В В Bring him to poise. There is no other way.

В В В В And yet what days were those, Parmenides!
В В В В When we were young, when we could number friends
В В В В In all the Italian cities like ourselves,
В В В В When with elated hearts we joinпїЅd your train,
В В В В Ye Sun-born Virgins! on the road of truth.
В В В В Then we could still enjoy, then neither thought
В В В В Nor outward things were closпїЅd and dead to us,
В В В В But we receivпїЅd the shock of mighty thoughts
В В В В On simple minds with a pure natural joy;
В В В В And if the sacred load oppressпїЅd our brain,
В В В В We had the power to feel the pressure eased,
В В В В The brow unbound, the thoughts flow free again,
В В В В In the delightful commerce of the world.
В В В В We had not lost our balance then, nor grown
В В В В ThoughtпїЅs slaves, and dead to every natural joy!
В В В В The smallest thing could give us pleasure then!
В В В В The sports of the country people,
В В В В A flute-note from the woods
В В В В Sunset over the sea;
В В В В Seed-time and harvest,
В В В В The reapers in the corn,
В В В В The vinedresser in his vineyard,
В В В В The village-girl at her wheel!

В В В В Fullness of life and power of feeling, ye
В В В В Are for the happy, for the souls at ease,
В В В В Who dwell on a firm basis of content!,
В В В В But he, who has outlivпїЅd his prosperous days,
В В В В But he, whose youth fell on a different world
В В В В From that on which his exiled age is thrown,
В В В В Whose mind was fed on other food, was trainпїЅd
В В В В By other rules than are in vogue to-day,
В В В В Whose habit of thought is fixпїЅd, who will not change,
В В В В But in a world he loves not must subsist
В В В В In ceaseless opposition, be the guard
В В В В Of his own breast, fetterd to what he guards,
В В В В That the world win no mastery over him;
В В В В Who has no friend, no fellow left, not one;
В В В В Who has no minuteпїЅs breathing space allowпїЅd
В В В В To nurse his dwindling faculty of joy,
В В В В Joy and the outward world must die to him,
В В В В As they are dead to me!
В В В В [A long pause, during which EMPEDOCLES remains
В В В В motionless, plunged in thought. The night deepens.
В В В В He moves forward and gazes round him, and proceeds:,

В В В В And you, ye stars,
В В В В Who slowly begin to marshal,
В В В В As of old, in the fields of heaven,
В В В В Your distant, melancholy lines!
В В В В Have you, too, survived yourselves?
В В В В Are you, too, what I fear to become?
В В В В You, too, once lived!
В В В В You too moved joyfully
В В В В Among august companions
В В В В In an older world, peopled by Gods,
В В В В In a mightier order,
В В В В The radiant, rejoicing, intelligent Sons of Heaven
В В В В But now, you kindle
В В В В Your lonely, cold-shining lights,
В В В В Unwilling lingerers
В В В В In the heavenly wilderness,
В В В В For a younger, ignoble world;
В В В В And renew, by necessity,
В В В В Night after night your courses,
В В В В In echoing unnearпїЅd silence,
В В В В Above a race you know not.
В В В В Uncaring and undelighted.
В В В В Without friend and without home;
В В В В Weary like us, though not
В В В В Weary with our weariness.

В В В В No, no, ye stars! there is no death with you,
В В В В No languor, no decay! Languor and death,
В В В В They are with me, not you! ye are alive!
В В В В Ye and the pure dark ether where ye ride
В В В В Brilliant above me! And thou, fiery world,
В В В В That sappпїЅst the vitals of this terrible mount
В В В В Upon whose charrпїЅd and quaking crust I stand,
В В В В Thou, too, brimmest with life!, the sea of cloud
В В В В That heaves its white and billowy vapours up
В В В В To moat this isle of ashes from the world,
В В В В Lives!, and that other fainter sea, far down,
В В В В OпїЅer whose lit floor a road of moonbeams leads
В В В В To EtnaпїЅs LiparпїЅan sister-fires
В В В В And the long dusky line of Italy,
В В В В That mild and luminous floor of waters lives,
В В В В With held-in joy swelling its heart!, I only,
В В В В Whose spring of hope is dried, whose spirit has failпїЅd,
В В В В I, who have not, like these, in solitude
В В В В MaintainпїЅd courage and force, and in myself
В В В В Nursed an immortal vigour, I alone
В В В В Am dead to life and joy; therefore I read
В В В В In all things my own deadness.
В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В [A long silence. He continues:,

В В В В Oh that I could glow like this mountain!
В В В В Oh that my heart bounded with the swell of the sea!
В В В В Oh that my soul were full of light as the stars!
В В В В Oh that it brooded over the world like the air!

В В В В But no, this heart will glow no more! thou art
В В В В A living man no more, Empedocles!
В В В В Nothing but a devouring flame of thought,
В В В В But a naked, eternally restless mind!
В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В  [After a pause:,

В В В В To the elements it came from
В В В В Everything will return.
В В В В Our bodies to earth,
В В В В Our blood to water,
В В В В Heat to fire,
В В В В Breath to air.
В В В В They were well born, they will be well entombпїЅd!
В В В В But mind?. . .

В В В В And we might gladly share the fruitful stir
В В В В Down in our mother earthпїЅs miraculous womb!
В В В В Well might it be
В В В В With what rollпїЅd of us in the stormy main!
В В В В We might have joy, blent with the all-bathing air,
В В В В Or with the nimble radiant life of fire!

В В В В But mind, but thought,
В В В В If these have been the master part of us,
В В В В Where will they find their parent element?
В В В В What will receive them, who will call them home?
В В В В But we shall still be in them, and they in us.
В В В В And we shall be the strangers of the world,
В В В В And they will be our lords, as they are now;
В В В В And keep us prisoners of our consciousness.
В В В В And never let us clasp and feel the All
В В В В But through their forms, and modes, and stifling veils.
В В В В And we shall be unsatisfied as now,
В В В В And we shall feel the agony of thirst,
В В В В The ineffable longing for the life of life
В В В В Baffled for ever: and still thought and mind
В В В В Will hurry us with them on their homeless march,
В В В В Over the unallied unopening earth,
В В В В Over the unrecognizing sea; while air
В В В В Will blow us fiercely back to sea and earth,
В В В В And fire repel us from its living waves.
В В В В And then we shall unwillingly return
В В В В Back to this meadow of calamity,
В В В В This uncongenial place, this human life;
В В В В And in our individual human state
В В В В Go through the sad probation all again,
В В В В To see if we will poise our life at last,
В В В В To see if we will now at last be true
В В В В To our own only true, deep-buried selves,
В В В В Being one with which we are one with the whole world;
В В В В Or whether we will once more fall away
В В В В Into some bondage of the flesh or mind,
В В В В Some slough of sense, or some fantastic maze
В В В В ForgпїЅd by the imperious lonely thinking-power.
В В В В And each succeeding age in which we are born
В В В В Will have more peril for us than the last;
В В В В Will goad our senses with a sharper spur,
В В В В Will fret our minds to an intenser play,
В В В В Will make ourselves harder to be discernпїЅd.
В В В В And we shall struggle awhile, gasp and rebel;
В В В В And we shall fly for refuge to past times,
В В В В Their soul of unworn youth, their breath of greatness;
В В В В And the reality will pluck us back,
В В В В Knead us in its hot hand, and change our nature.
В В В В And we shall feel our powers of effort flag,
В В В В And rally them for one last fight, and fail;
В В В В And we shall sink in the impossible strife,
В В В В And be astray for ever.
В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В Slave of sense
В В В В I have in no wise been; but slave of thought?,
В В В В And who can say:, I have been always free,
В В В В Lived ever in the light of my own soul?,
В В В В I cannot! I have lived in wrath and gloom,
В В В В Fierce, disputatious, ever at war with man,
В В В В Far from my own soul, far from warmth and light.
В В В В But I have not grown easy in these bonds,
В В В В But I have not denied what bonds these were!
В В В В Yea, I take myself to witness,
В В В В That I have loved no darkness,
В В В В Sophisticated no truth,
В В В В Nursed no delusion,
В В В В AllowпїЅd no fear!

В В В В And therefore, O ye elements, I know,
В В В В Ye know it too, it hath been granted me
В В В В Not to die wholly, not to be all enslavпїЅd.
В В В В I feel it in this hour! The numbing cloud
В В В В Mounts off my soul; I feel it, I breathe free!

В В В В Is it but for a moment?
В В В В Ah! boil up, ye vapours!
В В В В Leap and roar, thou sea of fire!
В В В В My soul glows to meet you.
В В В В Ere it flag, ere the mists
В В В В Of despondency and gloom
В В В В Rush over it again,
В В В В Receive me! Save me!В В В В [He plunges into the crater.

В В В В CALLICLES (front below)

В В В В Through the black, rushing smoke-bursts,
В В В В Thick breaks the red flame;
В В В В All Etna heaves fiercely
В В В В Her forest-clothпїЅd frame.

В В В В Not here, O Apollo
В В В В Are haunts meet for thee.
В В В В But, where Helicon breaks down
В В В В In cliff to the sea,

В В В В Where the moon-silverпїЅd inlets
В В В В Send far their light voice
В В В В Up the still vale of Thisbe,
В В В В O speed, and rejoice!

В В В В On the sward at the cliff-top
В В В В Lie strewn the white flocks;
В В В В On the cliff-side the pigeons
В В В В Roost deep in the rocks.

В В В В In the moonlight the shepherds,
В В В В Soft lullпїЅd by the rills,
В В В В Lie wrapt in their blankets,
В В В В Asleep on the hills.

В В В В , What forms are these coming
В В В В So white through the gloom:пїЅ
В В В В What garments out-glistening
В В В В The gold-flowerпїЅd broom?

В В В В What sweet-breathing presence
В В В В Out-perfumes the thyme?
В В В В What voices enrapture
В В В В The nightпїЅs balmy prime?,

В В В В пїЅTis Apollo comes leading
В В В В His choir, the Nine.
В В В В , The leader is fairest,
В В В В But all are divine.

В В В В They are lost in the hollows!
В В В В They stream up again!
В В В В What seeks on this mountain
В В В В The glorified train?,

В В В В They bathe on this mountain,
В В В В In the spring by their road;
В В В В Then on to Olympus,
В В В В Their endless abode!

В В В В , Whose praise do they mention
В В В В Of what is it told?,
В В В В What will be for ever;
В В В В What was from of old.

В В В В First hymn they the Father
В В В В Of all things; and then
В В В В The rest of immortals,
В В В В The action of men.

В В В В The day in his hotness,
В В В В The strife with the palm;
В В В В The night in her silence,
В В В В The stars in their calm.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Matthew Arnold represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.
